1: wayhî lᵉʿêt tᵉšûḇat haššānāh lᵉʿêt ṣēʾt hammᵉlāḵîm — “at the time of the return of the year, the time when kings go out.” This formula (identical to 2 Samuel 11:1) marks the spring campaign season when armies mustered after winter. The phrase tᵉšûḇat haššānāh (turn/return of the year) refers to the agricultural new year's onset after the spring rains. The Chronicler reproduces this verse from Samuel but omits everything that follows it in Samuel — the entire Bathsheba/Uriah narrative (2 Samuel 11:2–1 Chronicles 12:25) — moving directly from Joab's siege to the final capture of Rabbah. This omission is the Chronicler's most consequential redaction: David's great moral failure simply does not appear in Chronicles.
2: David takes the crown (keter) from the head of Milcom (malkām). The noun malkām is ambiguous: it can mean “their king” (from melek + 3mp suffix) or it can be the name of the Ammonite deity Milcom/Molech. Many translators read “their king,” but the Septuagint and Vulgate read “Milcom” (the god), and the crown's weight — ûmišqālô kikkār zāhāḇ, a talent of gold (about 75 lbs) — is appropriate for a divine statue's crown rather than a human king's. David also takes the valuable items (šālāl) of the city. The crown placed on David's head (wayyᵉhî ʿal rōʾš dāwid) symbolizes the defeat of Ammon's patron deity and David's sovereign inheritance.
3: The treatment of the Ammonites: wᵉʾet-hāʿām ʾăšer bāh hôṣîʾ wayyāśar bamagēr ûbamaḥărēšôt habarzel ûbammagrēzôt — he brought out the people that were in it and put them to work with saws and with iron picks and with axes (magrēzôt). The 2 Samuel 12:31 parallel has been much debated — whether the verbs mean “he put them to saws” (execution by sawing) or “he made them work with saws” (forced labor in stonecutting). The Chronicler's wording, with the hiphil yaʿăḇor (“passed/set them to”), is most naturally read as labor conscription for building projects, consistent with Solomon's later corvée practices.
4: The first of three Philistine giant encounters that form the chapter's second section (vv4-8): wayhî ʾaḥărê-ḵên wattaqom milḥāmāh bᵉGezer — thereafter battle arose at Gezer. Sibbᵉḵay haḥûšātî hikkāh ʾet-Sippay min-yaldê hārāpāʾ — Sibbecai the Hushathite struck down Sippai (spelled Ishbi-benob in 2 Samuel 21:16). The formula min-yaldê hārāpāʾ — “from the offspring of Rapha” — marks these as descendants of the Rephaim (pre-Israelite giants, cf. Deuteronomy 2:11, 20; 1 Chronicles 3:11). Rāpāʾ or hārāpāʾ is either a personal name (an ancestor of Philistine giants) or a class noun (the Rapha, the giants).
5: The second encounter: wattehî-ʿôd milḥāmāh ʾet-Pᵉlištîm wayyak Elḥānān ben-Yāʿîr ʾet-Laḥmî ʾăḥî Golyāt haGittî — Elhanan son of Jair killed Lahmi the brother of Goliath the Gittite. The significant phrase is ʾăḥî Golyāt — the brother of Goliath — which resolves an apparent conflict with 2 Samuel 21:19, where the parallel verse reads that Elhanan “killed Goliath the Gittite.” Chronicles clarifies: it was Goliath's brother, not Goliath himself (already killed by David in 1 Sam 17). The Chronicler's text either represents an independent tradition or is a theologically-motivated clarification preserving David's unique role as Goliath's killer.
6: The third encounter: wattehî-ʿôd milḥāmāh bᵉGat wayhî ʾîš middāh wᵉʾeṣbᵉʿōtāyw šēš-wāšēš ʿeśrîm wᵉʾarbaʿ — and there was again war at Gath, where there was a man of great stature, with six fingers on each hand and six toes on each foot, twenty-four in all. The formula ûhûʾ yulad lᵉhārāpāʾ — “and he also was born to the Rapha” — connects him to the giant lineage. Jonathan son of Shimea (David's brother) killed him. The six-fingered, six-toed condition (polydactyly) may have been regarded as a mark of the Rephaim.
7: wayyᵉḥārēp ʾet-yiśrāʾēl wayyakkēhû Yônātān ben-Šimᵉʾā ʾăḥî dāwid — the giant taunted (ḥārap, piel: to reproach, taunt, defy) Israel, and Jonathan son of Shimea, David's brother, struck him down. The verb ḥārap (to taunt/defy) was used of Goliath defying Israel's ranks (1 Samuel 17:10, 25, 26, 36, 45) — a deliberate thematic echo. The name Jonathan (= yᵉhônātān, “YHWH has given”) is the same as David's covenant friend, though a different individual.
8: Summary formula: ʾēlleh yullᵉdû lᵉhārāpāʾ bᵉGat wayyippᵉlû bᵉyad dāwid ûbᵉyad ʿăḇādāyw — “these were born to Rapha in Gath and they fell by the hand of David and by the hand of his servants.” The formula nāpal bᵉyad (to fall into/by the hand of) denotes defeat in battle. The fourfold giant-killing sequence — Goliath (1 Sam 17), then three more in this chapter — establishes that the elimination of the giant threat to Israel was accomplished through David's reign and legacy. For the Chronicler, this is another necessary condition for the temple: the land secured, the giants eliminated.
